1: A man of the house of Levi married a daughter of Levi — the specific names (Amram and Jochebed) are withheld here and revealed later (Ex Exodus 6:20; Numbers 26:59). The sparse genealogy focuses the reader on the event rather than the persons. The Levitical parentage of Moses is theologically significant: the future mediator of the covenant will come from the tribe that later receives the priestly office. Aaron (the first high priest) and Moses (the lawgiver/mediator) are brothers from the same tribe, positioning Levi as the tribe of both sacerdotal and prophetic mediation.
2: She hid him for three months — the three-month concealment represents the limits of domestic resistance to state power. Under Pharaoh's decree, the birth of a male child required immediate action. The mother's act of hiding the child was an act of civil defiance, at personal risk. 'She saw that he was good' (vatere oto ki-tov) echoes the creation refrain of Gen 1 (ki tov) — the child's goodness is recognized with the same formula used when God saw the light and pronounced it good. The echo is not accidental: a new creation, a new beginning, is observed by a mother's eyes.
3: The papyrus basket (tevah of gome) coated with bitumen and pitch — tevah is the same word used only twice in the OT: for Noah's ark (Gen 6-9) and for this basket. The parallel is structural: in both cases, a righteous individual is preserved in a vessel on threatening waters while the world outside is threatened with death. The materials — bitumen (chemar) and pitch (zepheth) — are the same waterproofing materials Noah used (Genesis 6:14). The Nile becomes the flood; Moses becomes a new Noah; the bulrushes become the new Ararat. Egypt is the waters of chaos; the vessel carries the one through whom salvation will come.
4: His sister (identified in Ex 15:20 as Miriam) stationed herself at a distance to observe — letedah mah-yea'aseh lo, to see what would happen to him. The sister's watchful presence is the hinge of the narrative: she observes, then initiates (Exodus 2:7). Miriam, who will later lead Israel in song at the Reed Sea (Ex Exodus 15:20-21), here plays her first decisive role in the preservation of the deliverer. The narrative quietly assigns agency to an unnamed girl before the major characters have names.
5: Pharaoh's daughter came down to bathe at the Nile — Egyptian queens and noblewomen are depicted in Egyptian art bathing in the Nile or in palace pools. The scene is historically plausible. The daughter of the man who ordered the genocide discovers the basket hidden among the reeds. Providence brings together the most vulnerable (a condemned infant) and the most powerful (a daughter of absolute power) at the same point in the river. She recognizes the child as a Hebrew infant — the very child her father's decree condemned — and chooses compassion over compliance.
6: She had compassion on him (vatachmol alav) — chamal is the word for sparing or having pity, the same word used when Saul spared Agag (1 Samuel 15:9) and when YHWH says 'should I not spare Nineveh' (Jonah 4:11). A daughter of the Pharaoh who commanded genocide spares the condemned child — the political order is undermined from within by the compassion of its own household. She identifies him immediately as a Hebrew child: no illusions, but compassion stronger than political calculation.
7: Miriam steps forward and offers to find a Hebrew nursing woman — the initiative is swift and bold for a young girl addressing a princess. The offer is transparent in retrospect: the nurse will be the child's own mother. But the scene is presented with dramatic irony: Pharaoh's daughter accepts the offer, not knowing she is agreeing to reunite mother and child. The arrangement is providential: the decree of death is reversed by the decree of the powerful, through the initiative of the powerless.
8: 'Go' — Pharaoh's daughter authorizes Miriam with a single word. The girl fetches the child's own mother. The speed of the exchange and the lightness of the narrative conceal the enormity of what has happened: the infant condemned to death by Pharaoh's decree will be raised in Pharaoh's household, nursed by his own mother, at Pharaoh's expense. The very mechanism of oppression funds the upbringing of Israel's deliverer.
9: 'Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages' — the irony is complete. Egypt will pay an Israelite mother to raise her own son within Pharaoh's court. The wages (sakar) that Egypt denied the enslaved Israelites (there is no record of payment for the building-project labor) are here given to the mother of the future deliverer. The reversal is personal before it becomes national: Jochebed is compensated by the household that oppressed her people.
10: She named him Moses (Mosheh) — Egyptian: msy = 'born of' or 'son of' (as in Thutmoses = born of Thoth; Ramesses = born of Ra). The Egyptian name is given a Hebrew etymology: ki min-hammayim meshitihu — 'for I drew him out of the water' (from mashah = to draw out). Both etymologies are at play: the Egyptian child of the palace, and the Hebrew child drawn from the water. Acts 7:22 adds that Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians — palace education in the New Kingdom included language, administration, military training, religious knowledge, and scribal arts. Forty years of Egyptian formation prepares him for forty years of wilderness and forty years of leadership.
11: Moses grew up and went out to his brothers — the narrative compresses forty years into a phrase. The transition from palace to people is an act of identification: Moses chooses to look at the burden (sivelotam) of his people. He kills an Egyptian taskmaster who was beating a Hebrew. The killing is done in secret — he looks 'this way and that' — which reveals both the clandestine nature of the act and its moral ambiguity. Moses is not yet a deliverer but a man acting on impulse; his individual act of justice cannot deliver the people and nearly destroys him. The gap between the private act and public mandate is the gap the burning bush will bridge.
12: He hid the Egyptian in the sand — the concealment anticipates the next day's reversal when the act becomes known. Desert sand was used for hasty burials in antiquity, and Egypt's desert environment made such concealment possible. The image is not heroic: Moses is covering his tracks, not leading a revolution. The narrative is honest about the unsanctioned, impulsive, ineffective nature of Moses' first intervention — the contrast with the commissioned deliverer of Exodus 3 is stark.
13: Two Hebrews fighting — Moses intervenes again, challenging the one in the wrong. The response — 'Who made you a prince and a judge over us?' — is devastating in its logic: Moses has no authority, no commission, no recognized standing among his own people. The question echoes forward in Israel's history: the prophet without honor among his own (cf. Luke 4:24), the sent one rejected by those he was sent to serve. Stephen cites this exact moment in Acts 7:27, 35 as the prototype of Israel's rejection of its own deliverer — a pattern he traces from Moses to Jesus.
14: 'Did you intend to kill me as you killed the Egyptian?' — the act is known. The network of enslaved people was apparently more observant than Moses realized; secrets in a slave community are not secrets for long. The exposure of the killing and the rebuff from his own people collapse Moses' nascent project. He is now a wanted man in Egypt, rejected by the people he tried to help, without a home in either world. The social destruction is complete: he cannot return to Pharaoh's court, and he cannot lead the Hebrews. The only direction is out.
15: Moses fled to Midian — Midian is the territory east and southeast of the Sinai, in the northwestern Arabian Peninsula (modern northwest Saudi Arabia and southern Jordan). The Midianites were descendants of Abraham through Keturah (Genesis 25:1-2), making them distant kin of the Israelites with a shared Abrahamic heritage. The journey from the eastern Nile delta to Midian through the Sinai Peninsula was a trek of several weeks through desert terrain. Moses arrives as a fugitive and sits by a well — the well scene immediately invokes the patriarchal type-scene: Abraham's servant meets Rebekah at a well (Gen 24), Jacob meets Rachel at a well (Gen 29). The reader expects a marriage to follow.
16: The priest of Midian (identified as Reuel here, as Jethro in Ex 3:1 and elsewhere — possibly two names, a personal name and a title/honorific) had seven daughters. Reuel means 'friend of God' (re'u-El). A Midianite priest with this name suggests the Midianites had some knowledge of El, the high God of the Semitic world. The seven daughters are shepherdesses — their presence at a public well for watering flocks is a normal pastoral scene. Seven is the number of completeness; the arrival of seven daughters at the well creates a scene of providential abundance.
17: Shepherds drove the daughters away — the social dynamic is familiar in the ancient world: male shepherds asserting priority at a shared water source at the expense of women. Moses' intervention is immediate: he rose to their defense and watered their flock. The same protective instinct that killed the Egyptian taskmaster is here channeled constructively — a small act of justice at a foreign well. The contrast with the Egyptian incident is instructive: the same impulse, in a proportionate and non-lethal context, produces a good outcome. Moses is not an unjust man; he is a man whose justice needs a proper channel.
18: 'Why have you come home so early today?' — Reuel's question establishes the daughters' normal pattern: the well was a contested space where they were regularly delayed. Moses' intervention was noteworthy precisely because it reversed the usual social dynamic. Reuel's response to the account — 'Where is he? Why did you leave the man?' — reflects the Middle Eastern hospitality ethic: the stranger who has done a good deed must be brought in and fed. The invitation to eat is the beginning of Moses' forty-year residency in Midian.
19: 'An Egyptian man delivered us from the hand of the shepherds' — Zipporah and her sisters identify Moses as Egyptian, not Hebrew. Forty years in Pharaoh's court had given Moses the appearance, language, and dress of an Egyptian. His identity is visibly ambiguous: born Hebrew, raised Egyptian, fled to Midian. This triple alienation — belonging fully to no nation — is not a tragedy but a formation. The man who will stand between Pharaoh and Israel, mediating between two peoples, must be at home in neither world and known to both.
20: Reuel's hospitality invitation follows the ancient Near Eastern pattern: the stranger must not be allowed to remain unfed. The principle of hospitality to strangers (hachnasat orchim in later Jewish thought) was a fundamental social obligation in desert cultures where travelers were entirely dependent on the goodwill of settled households. Reuel's invitation is culturally instinctive and narratively providential: the man who will one day bring Israel to meet YHWH at Sinai first meets YHWH's people through a priestly household's table.
21: Moses agreed to dwell with the man and was given Zipporah (Tzipporah = bird, H6855) as his wife. The marriage follows the patriarchal pattern: the stranger who proves himself at a foreign well marries into the local family (Jacob married Rachel after demonstrating his strength at the well in Genesis 29:10-11). Moses' marriage to a Midianite connects him to the semi-nomadic pastoral world that was the original social environment of the patriarchs. Zipporah's significance extends beyond this chapter: she will play a decisive role in Ex Exodus 4:24-26 (the circumcision crisis), and her voice and actions matter in the narrative.
22: Gershom — Moses named his son 'stranger there' (ger sham), explaining: 'I have been a stranger in a foreign land.' The name encapsulates Moses' identity crisis: he is a Hebrew who was an Egyptian who is now in Midian. The word ger (stranger/sojourner) becomes one of the most important categories in Israelite law — the stranger who lives among you must be treated justly, 'for you were strangers in Egypt' (Ex Exodus 22:21, Exodus 23:9; Leviticus 19:34; Deuteronomy 10:19). Moses' forty years as a ger in Midian form him in the experience that will ground Israel's ethics of stranger-care.
23: During that long period — forty years pass. The king of Egypt died (whether Thutmose III, Seti I, or another depends on the dating). The Israelites groaned and cried out (vayiz'aqu — the legal term for a cry of appeal before a judge or authority). Their cry went up to God — the language is judicial: a formal complaint reaching the divine court. This is not merely prayer but a legal appeal. In the ANE, the oppressed could appeal to the king for justice; the Israelites, having no human king to appeal to, cry out to the divine judge. The cry is heard (Exodus 2:24).
24: God heard (shama), God remembered (zakar) his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. The covenant memory of YHWH is not passive recollection but active initiation — in Hebrew thought, to remember is to act on what is remembered. God's remembering of the patriarchal covenant is the theological engine of the Exodus: Israel is not delivered because it deserves it, but because God made a promise to the fathers and is faithful to his word. The triple invocation of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob recurs throughout Exodus and becomes the foundational formula of Israelite prayer.
25: God saw Israel (vayyar), and God knew (vayyeda). The four verbs across Exodus 2:24-25 — heard, remembered, saw, knew — constitute a fourfold statement of divine attention. Yada here is covenantal knowing, not mere cognitive awareness: the same word used for intimate relational knowledge (Genesis 4:1), for YHWH knowing Moses face to face (Deuteronomy 34:10), for the elect being foreknown (Amos 3:2). God does not merely observe Israel's suffering from a distance; he acknowledges it with covenant commitment. The Exodus is the consequence of these four acts of divine attention.
